Event: Gives up being a detective and takes a job as a regular patrol cop
Explanation: He sacrifices prestige to gain personal stability while still contributing productively as a cop.

Event: Refuses repeated offers to return to detective work, saying he’s happier walking the beat
Explanation: He protects his own wellbeing and still does useful work, though it withholds his top skills from major cases.

Event: Decides to rejoin Homicide when Marlo’s crew grows out of control
Explanation: He regains meaningful detective work and uses his skills to combat a serious criminal threat.

Event: Takes numerous jabs from others “with a smile”
Explanation: By absorbing teasing without escalation he protects his relationships and workplace harmony.

Event: Maintains functional performance at work despite alcoholism
Explanation: His addiction ruins his private life even as his work benefits from his obsessive dedication.

Event: Regularly drinks on the job in season 5
Explanation: His drinking erodes his own control and endangers cases and others without any real benefit.

Event: Executes the fake serial killer plan to get Marlo off the streets
Explanation: He gains a tactical win against Marlo but destroys his career and damages institutional integrity.

Event: Takes responsibility for Marlo via the serial killer scheme, getting him “off the streets (for now)”
Explanation: He meaningfully reduces immediate street violence but at the cost of his career and reputation.

Event: Denies responsibility with “What the fuck did I do?” when reprimanded
Explanation: Deflecting blame protects his ego short term while undermining accountability and frustrating others.

Event: Accepts guilt on realizing the ramifications of his actions and repeats the line in a guilty tone
Explanation: Recognizing his fault helps him internalize consequences and minimally repairs trust.

Event: Becomes obsessed with taking Stringer down as payback after he walks free
Explanation: His vendetta feeds his ego and focus but distorts priorities and risks collateral costs.

Event: Carefully builds a wire case and catches Stringer incriminating himself
Explanation: He uses skillful, lawful investigation to pin a major criminal, aiding both his standing and public safety.

Event: Becomes distraught when Stringer is murdered before he can be arrested
Explanation: He suffers emotionally while the city arguably benefits from Stringer’s removal.

Event: Regularly bends and breaks rules for information instead of using proper procedure
Explanation: He may gain marginal speed or satisfaction but breeds mistrust and needless institutional damage.

Event: Uses the same investigative methods as Bunk and Lester but breaks rules to do it
Explanation: He gains no extra benefit over colleagues yet incurs risk and undermines procedures.

Event: Refuses to “play the game” and puts doing his work above his own well-being and loyalty to supporters
Explanation: His stance protects his personal ideals while repeatedly damaging his career and allies who help him.

Event: Deserts MCU and works as a patrolman in season 4, getting his personal life back together
Explanation: He trades prestige for stability and healthier relationships while still serving effectively.

Event: Returns his focus to the Stanfield case at the end of season 4
Explanation: He re-engages his investigative talents against a serious target, advancing both his aims and public safety.

Event: Drinks heavily and resumes womanizing in season 5 despite living with Beadie
Explanation: He indulges addiction that hurts his relationship and stability for fleeting gratification.

Event: Hatches the fake serial killer plot as an outlet for his ego and desire to matter
Explanation: The scheme flatters his self-image briefly while gravely endangering careers, budgets, and trust.

Event: Gives everything he has to cases he cares about, ignoring rules and self-preservation
Explanation: His obsessive drive solves hard cases and helps others, though at real long-term cost to himself.

Event: Tries to independently solve the dead girls case to show off to Lester and Bunk while bored in Marine unit
Explanation: He does extra work from boredom and ego, gaining nothing new while modestly aiding an already-handled case.

Event: Drinks to drown his sorrows whenever distraught
Explanation: He self-medicates emotional pain at significant personal cost with no benefit to anyone else.

Event: Drives drunk at times
Explanation: He recklessly endangers himself and others for trivial convenience while intoxicated.

Event: Refuses to engage in police brutality despite many lines he does cross
Explanation: He protects his own legal exposure and preserves others’ rights by keeping violence in check.

Event: Lets his alcoholism and violations lead to his eventual expulsion from the force
Explanation: His accumulated misconduct ends his career, though institutions arguably benefit from his removal.

Event: Rekindles his relationship with Beadie at the end
Explanation: He regains emotional support and offers her renewed partnership, moving both toward a healthier life.

Event: Brings the homeless man he stashed in Virginia back to Baltimore
Explanation: He partly repairs his earlier abuse of the man and facilitates his access to proper help.

Event: Loses sight of his children while having them tail Stringer in a market
Explanation: He gains questionable investigative help while seriously endangering his kids.

Event: Leaves his kids alone at night to have a quickie in a hotel
Explanation: He risks their safety for momentary sexual gratification, hurting them and ultimately himself.

Event: Poses as a bigot with a sheriff, assuming the man is prejudiced
Explanation: He tries to manipulate cooperation through feigned racism, benefiting his case while insulting the target.

Event: Backpedals when he learns the sheriff’s wife is black
Explanation: He repairs a misjudgment quickly, limiting damage to the relationship at manageable cost to his pride.

Event: Gets demoted at the end of season 1 after helping bring the Barksdales to justice
Explanation: His effective work improves public safety but harms his own career.

Event: Is again punished after bringing Marlo’s crew to justice in season 5
Explanation: His results benefit the city, yet he personally loses his job and future in policing.

Event: Goes out of his way to pursue justice for the thirteen “Jane Does” when no one else cares
Explanation: He expends time and emotional energy to dignify marginalized victims, aiding both them and his sense of purpose.

Event: Goes to pick up the homeless man he had stashed in Virginia to get him help
Explanation: He actively undoes part of his exploitative scheme and steers the man toward assistance.

Event: Accepts reassignment to the Marine unit and despises it
Explanation: He passively endures a punishment post that makes him miserable without helping anyone.

Event: Finds a way from the boat to shove a major case into Rawls’ jurisdiction “for the fun of it”
Explanation: He gains satisfaction and leverage by dumping work on Rawls at the cost of interdepartmental goodwill.

Event: Has sex with his ex Elena once and assumes the divorce is temporary
Explanation: He indulges false hope and emotional entanglement that ultimately hurts him more than anyone gains.

Event: Drops out of college and marries Elena after she gets pregnant
Explanation: He sacrifices educational prospects to assume responsibility for his family, which benefits them and gives him a stable role.

Event: Willingly stabs Daniels in the back via Colvin despite Daniels helping him off the boat
Explanation: He pushes his investigative agenda at the expense of a key ally’s trust and career security.

Event: Poses as a British businessman to go undercover in a brothel
Explanation: His ruse advances the operation and his case work with minimal collateral harm.

Event: Is cool about Daniels dating Rhonda while still working with her
Explanation: He manages his jealousy, preserving professional cooperation and their personal happiness.

Event: Accepts being lead investigator on the fake Serial Killer case he himself invented
Explanation: He deepens his own legal and career jeopardy while misdirecting institutional resources he created the need for.

Event: Capably diverts serial-killer case resources into real police work
Explanation: He transforms misallocated funds into genuine investigative gains, benefiting himself and others despite the rotten premise.

Event: Drags Bunk, Lester, and Sydnor into his illegal schemes, compromising their integrity
Explanation: He gains support for his plot while risking his friends’ careers and ethics.
